title: Split Trigger Systems Into Domain Folders
summary: Large trigger projects should organize gameplay into system folders such as diplomacy, provinces, resources, quests, abilities, and world logic instead of keeping all trigger construction in one file.
when_to_use: planning a new trigger-heavy project
refactoring a giant scenario builder into modules
explaining how to isolate gameplay systems
key_points: Use folders as feature boundaries.
Keep orchestration at the top and detailed logic inside each system folder.
Make it possible to reason about one gameplay system without opening unrelated logic.
pitfalls: A folder per feature is not enough if each folder still contains one giant file.
Do not mix unrelated systems into one orchestration module.

title: Use Hierarchical apply_ Functions
summary: Trigger and XS projects scale better when one top-level entrypoint calls major apply_ functions, which then call narrower apply_ functions for sub-systems and leaf behaviors.
when_to_use: explaining project orchestration
making systems callable in and out
decomposing large trigger builders
key_points: Top-level entrypoint should be thin.
System-level apply_ modules should map to meaningful gameplay domains.
Leaf modules should handle specific trigger effects, spell behavior, or region logic.
pitfalls: Do not let the top-level entrypoint become the whole implementation.
Do not call deeply unrelated systems from leaf modules.

title: Centralize Scenario Variable Allocation In aoe_variables
summary: Use a dedicated aoe_variables layer to allocate scenario variables once, assign stable human-readable names, and expose mappings to downstream systems.
when_to_use: adding many trigger variables
explaining how to avoid variable ID collisions
designing shared registries for trigger systems
key_points: Create variables centrally.
Store variable IDs in shared mappings.
Let systems consume mappings instead of allocating IDs independently.
pitfalls: Do not let each feature allocate scenario variable IDs independently.
Do not hide the variable naming scheme across many unrelated files.

title: Use dictionary.py As The Balance Layer
summary: Store spell values, progression numbers, thresholds, descriptions, and other balancing data in dictionary.py files while keeping mutation logic in separate modules.
when_to_use: explaining data-driven balancing
refactoring hardcoded numbers out of trigger logic
teaching how to separate configuration from code
key_points: Use dictionary.py for tunable values and balance data.
Use logic modules for trigger construction and lifecycle handling.
Open dictionary files first when the task is balancing.
pitfalls: Do not bury balance values directly inside long trigger-building loops.
Do not confuse balance dictionaries with the actual logic implementation.

title: Centralize XS Variable Declaration And Naming
summary: Projects that mix triggers and XS should declare or generate XS variables in one place, emit persistent extern definitions there, and expose semantic mappings so feature modules do not hardcode scattered variable IDs.
when_to_use: designing XS variable registries
connecting triggers to XS state
explaining extern variable declarations
key_points: Keep XS variable declaration centralized.
Emit persistent scenario-style XS globals with extern declarations such as extern int xsVariableN = -1;.
Map semantic names to XS variable slots in shared structures.
Only globally fixed bridge constants should be hardcoded broadly.
implementation_steps: Create one XS-variable owner module for the project.
Generate or declare the extern int xsVariableN = -1; block there.
Store a semantic mapping from gameplay meaning to generated XS variable names.
Expose helper functions so feature modules request variables by meaning instead of by raw slot.
agent_instructions: Keep XS globals and trigger variables conceptually separate.
Use xsTriggerVariable(...) and xsSetTriggerVariable(...) for shared trigger state, not plain XS globals.
pitfalls: Do not create ad hoc XS variable names in many unrelated modules.
Do not mix trigger variable IDs and XS variable slots as if they were the same registry.

title: Generate XS In Ordered Phases
summary: A practical XS build pipeline writes or assembles persistent extern variable definitions first, then adds shared helper functions, then appends or assembles the feature-specific XS functions for the project.
when_to_use: teaching how generated xs files are built
refactoring a single giant xs writer into phases
explaining why variable definitions, helper code, and runtime code should be separated
key_points: Write or assemble the extern variable block first.
Keep shared helper functions separate from feature-specific runtime functions.
Assemble runtime XS in phases instead of mixing every concern into one writer.
implementation_steps: Generate the extern-variable definitions and semantic mapping.
Register or append shared helper functions such as compare and change-variable helpers.
Register or append feature-specific function templates.
Assemble the final runtime XS body after support content is ready.
agent_instructions: If the project already follows a direct-write pipeline, preserve the phased order instead of collapsing everything into one loop.
If the project uses an include registry, keep include filenames relative and let the assembly step join them.
query_terms: write extern variables then append functions
pitfalls: Do not append helper templates blindly without deduplication checks when the project already guards against duplicates.
Do not scatter XS file generation across many unrelated gameplay modules.

title: Treat XS Output Destination As Configuration
summary: The destination of the generated XS file should come from project configuration, build arguments, or an explicit user decision; reusable guidance should never hardcode a machine-specific local mod path.
when_to_use: deciding where the generated xs file should be placed
generalizing a local project into publishable guidance
teaching agents not to invent local machine paths
key_points: Do not hardcode a PC-specific absolute output path in reusable project guidance.
Use project config or build-time parameters for the final XS destination.
Ask the user where to place the generated .xs file when the destination is not already defined.

path_guidance.support_include_files: Keep generated include filenames relative inside XS, for example include "support_variables.xs";.
path_guidance.final_output_destination: Use a project-defined mod or scenario XS output location if configured; otherwise ask the user for the exact destination before writing.

agent_instructions: Search the project for an existing XS output configuration before asking the user.
If no configuration exists, ask the user where to create the generated .xs file.
Do not substitute a guessed machine path.
query_terms: where to place generated xs file
where should the xs file go
pitfalls: Do not encode one developer PC path as if it were the general rule.
Do not confuse relative include filenames with the final absolute or project-relative destination chosen by the build.

title: Validate Generated XS As A Separate Workflow
summary: Generated XS should be assembled and validated through a dedicated validation step instead of assuming that builder output is always correct.
when_to_use: teaching XS generation workflows
separating scenario build from XS validation
explaining post-generation checks
key_points: Generate XS in one step and validate it in another.
Persist generated XS to a file before validation when the validator expects file input.
Treat validation as part of the development loop, not an optional afterthought.
pitfalls: Do not rely only on visual inspection for generated XS.
Do not hide validation inside unrelated scenario generation code.
